regions: Appalachia: WV, KY, VA, PA, TN, OH, AL
Western coalfields: WY, CO, UT, MT

what_they_did.daily_work: Extracted coal from underground mines by drilling, blasting, and loading coal into mine cars. Before mechanization, miners used hand drills, black powder (later dynamite), picks, and shovels. A miner typically worked a 'room' — an assigned section of the coal seam — loading 3-6 tons of coal per shift. Work was performed in near-total darkness except for the lamp on the miner's cap.
what_they_did.seasonal_cycle: Coal mining was year-round, but demand peaked in fall and winter heating months (October-March). Summer often brought short weeks or temporary layoffs. Company towns all but shut down during slack periods.
tools_and_equipment: Cap lamp (open flame, then carbide, then electric)
what_they_did.typical_hours: 8-10 hours underground per shift. Under the 1898 bituminous coal agreements, the 8-hour day became standard in union mines, though actual time underground often exceeded this.

economics.typical_arrangement: Most miners were paid by the ton of coal loaded — typically $0.40-$0.80 per ton in the early 1900s. The company weighed the coal and deducted for rock, slack, and other impurities. Day wages (for timbering, track-laying, and other non-loading work) were $2.50-$4.00 per day in the 1910s-1920s.
economics.annual_income_range: 1910s: $500-$800/year (full-time work was rare). 1920s: $800-$1,200/year. Union mines 1940s-1950s: $3,000-$5,000/year. Earnings were highly variable because of short weeks, strikes, and seasonal demand.
economics.comparison: A coal miner in 1920 could earn $4-$5 per day when working — competitive with factory wages — but irregular employment meant annual earnings were often 30-40% lower than those of a full-time factory worker.
economics.debt_cycle: Company stores (the 'pluck me' system) extended credit against future earnings at inflated prices. Miners were paid in scrip redeemable only at the company store. The phrase 'I owe my soul to the company store' from Merle Travis's 1946 song captured a lived reality.

hazards: Roof falls (the leading killer — responsible for roughly half of mine fatalities)
Haulage accidents from mine cars and machinery
working_conditions.living_conditions: In company towns, the mining company owned every structure — houses, the store, the church, the school. Houses were typically 3-4 room frame structures built in identical rows. Miners paid rent deducted from their wages. Eviction was immediate upon termination or during strikes.
working_conditions.family_involvement: Women did not work underground (by law in most states from the 1880s) but maintained households in difficult conditions — hauling water, growing gardens, taking in boarders. Boys as young as 10 worked as breaker boys (sorting coal above ground) or door boys (opening ventilation doors underground) before child labor reforms.
working_conditions.autonomy: Underground, skilled miners had considerable autonomy — they chose where to drill, how to set charges, and managed their own rooms. Above ground, in company towns, autonomy was minimal. The company controlled housing, shopping, schools, and even which candidates appeared on election ballots.

push_factors: Mine closures as the industry contracted (especially after 1950)
Mechanization (continuous miners and longwall machines) eliminated hand-loading jobs
Black lung disease and injuries forced early retirement
Labor wars and blacklisting after strikes
why_they_left.what_replaced_them: Continuous mining machines, longwall shearers, and surface (strip) mining methods eliminated most underground hand-loading jobs. Employment in US coal mining fell from 700,000 in 1923 to under 150,000 by 1970.
why_they_left.narrative_hook: When {ancestor_name} came out of the mines in {county} for the last time around {year}, the decision likely wasn't sudden — it was the slow accumulation of coughing fits, short paychecks, and the knowledge that the seam was playing out.

records_generated.record_type: Mine employment records and payroll ledgers
records_generated.what_it_shows: Names, hire dates, job classifications, tonnage loaded, wages earned, store account debits
records_generated.where_to_find: West Virginia State Archives, Eastern Kentucky University Special Collections, University of Pittsburgh Archives, state mine inspector records
records_generated.research_tip: Many mine records were lost when companies closed. State mine inspector annual reports, which listed mine employees or fatalities, are often a more reliable surviving source.

records_generated.what_it_shows: Name of victim, age, nationality, cause of death or injury, mine name and location, date of accident
records_generated.where_to_find: State mine inspector reports (published annually by coal-producing states), US Bureau of Mines records at NARA
records_generated.research_tip: If an ancestor died in a mine accident, the state inspector's report will likely include details not found in a death certificate. Major disasters generated extensive federal investigation reports.

records_generated.record_type: Company town records (rent rolls, store ledgers)
records_generated.what_it_shows: Family names, house assignments, monthly rent, store purchases and debts
records_generated.where_to_find: University archives in coal-producing states, county historical societies
records_generated.research_tip: Company store ledgers can reveal details of daily life — what families ate, what they wore, when they bought school supplies — that no other record captures

census_clues.what_to_look_for: Look for clusters of miners on the same census page — this indicates a mining camp or company town. If the household head is a coal miner and the home is listed as 'Rented,' they likely lived in a company house.
census_clues.red_flags: A miner who appears in one coal county in 1910 and a different coal county in 1920 probably followed open mines. If a miner disappears from coal country entirely and reappears in an industrial city (Detroit, Akron, Cleveland), they likely left mining for factory work.

cultural_context: Coal mining created some of the most isolated, tightly-knit, and combative communities in American history. The mine wars of the early 20th century — Matewan, Blair Mountain, Harlan County — were not simply labor disputes but armed conflicts over the right of workers to organize and to live outside the total control of the coal company. Mining culture produced distinctive music, foodways, and a fierce independence that migrants carried to the cities they moved to.

counter_narratives.claim: Coal miners were not exclusively white Appalachian natives
counter_narratives.context: Southern coalfields employed significant numbers of Black miners — in Alabama's Birmingham district, Black miners were often the majority. Immigrant miners from Italy, Hungary, Poland, and Wales worked alongside native-born whites. West Virginia's coalfields were among the most ethnically diverse workplaces in early 20th-century America.

counter_narratives.claim: Not all coal towns were oppressive company towns
counter_narratives.context: Some mining communities, particularly in the Illinois and Indiana coalfields, were independent towns where miners owned their homes, shopped freely, and participated in civic life. The 'company town' model was most extreme in Appalachia — particularly in southern West Virginia and eastern Kentucky.

source_references.author: Corbin, David Alan
source_references.title: Life, Work, and Rebellion in the Coal Fields: The Southern West Virginia Miners, 1880-1922
source_references.notes: Foundational study of coal mining life and labor conflict in the most contested coalfield region in America

source_references.author: Fishback, Price V.
source_references.title: Soft Coal, Hard Choices: The Economic Welfare of Bituminous Coal Miners, 1890-1930
source_references.notes: Economic analysis with detailed wage and employment data essential for accurate narrative construction

source_references.author: Lewis, Ronald L.
source_references.title: Black Coal Miners in America: Race, Class, and Community Conflict, 1780-1980
source_references.notes: Comprehensive history of Black miners, essential for understanding the racial dimensions of coal mining

source_references.author: Shifflett, Crandall
source_references.title: Coal Towns: Life, Work, and Culture in Company Towns of Southern Appalachia, 1880-1960
source_references.notes: Detailed examination of daily life in company-owned mining towns
